content.problemStatement:
Alice has an array AAA of length NNN. Every element of AAA is either 111 or 222.

Alice will modify the array according to the following process:


First, choose a permutation PPP of the integers [1,2,…,N−1][1, 2, \ldots, N-1][1,2,…,N−1].

A permutation of the integers [1,2,…,N−1][1, 2, \ldots, N-1][1,2,…,N−1] is an array of length N−1N-1N−1 that contains every integer from 111 to N−1N-1N−1 exactly once.
Then, for each iii from 111 to N−1N-1N−1 in order:

Let x=Pix = P_ix=Pi​.
If Ax=1A_{x} = 1Ax​=1 and Ax+1=2A_{x + 1} = 2Ax+1​=2, swap AxA_{x}Ax​ and Ax+1A_{x + 1}Ax+1​.
Otherwise, do nothing.


Define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the number of **distinct** final arrays can Alice obtain by performing this process starting with AAA, across all choices of the permutation PPP.

Note that only arrays that appear at the end of the process, i.e. after all N−1N-1N−1 operations have been performed, are to be counted. Intermediate states do not count.

Compute the answer for each suffix of AAA, i.e. for each iii from 111 to NNN, compute

f([Ai,Ai+1,…,AN])f([A_i, A_{i+1}, \ldots, A_N])f([Ai​,Ai+1​,…,AN​]).

The answers can be large, so output them modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN – the length of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers: the answers for the suffixes starting at i=1,2,3,…,Ni = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, Ni=1,2,3,…,N in order.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array is such that no matter which suffix is chosen, and which permutation is chosen, no elements can be swapped. So, the answer for every suffix is 111.
Test case 222: Consider the entire array A=[1,2,1,2]A = [1, 2, 1, 2]A=[1,2,1,2], which is the suffix starting at index 111.
There are 666 choices for the permutation PPP. Going through them all,

P=[1,2,3]P = [1, 2, 3]P=[1,2,3]. Here,

For i=1i = 1i=1, P1P_1P1​ and P2P_2P2​ will swap. The array is [2,1,1,2][2, 1, 1, 2][2,1,1,2].
For i=2i = 2i=2, nothing changes.
For i=3i = 3i=3, P3P_3P3​ and P4P_4P4​ will swap. The array is [2,1,2,1][2, 1, 2, 1][2,1,2,1].


P=[1,3,2]P = [1, 3, 2]P=[1,3,2]. Here,

For i=1i = 1i=1, P1P_1P1​ and P2P_2P2​ will swap. The array is [2,1,1,2][2, 1, 1, 2][2,1,1,2].
For i=2i = 2i=2, P3P_3P3​ and P4P_4P4​ will swap. The array is [2,1,2,1][2, 1, 2, 1][2,1,2,1].
For i=3i = 3i=3, P2P_2P2​ and P3P_3P3​ will swap. The array is [2,2,1,1][2, 2, 1, 1][2,2,1,1].


P=[2,1,3]P = [2, 1, 3]P=[2,1,3]. The final array will be [2,1,2,1][2, 1, 2, 1][2,1,2,1].
P=[2,3,1]P = [2, 3, 1]P=[2,3,1]. The final array will be [2,1,2,1][2, 1, 2, 1][2,1,2,1].
P=[3,1,2]P = [3, 1, 2]P=[3,1,2]. The final array will be [2,2,1,1][2, 2, 1, 1][2,2,1,1].
P=[3,2,1]P = [3, 2, 1]P=[3,2,1]. The final array will be [2,1,2,1][2, 1, 2, 1][2,1,2,1].

So, the distinct final arrays are [2,1,2,1][2, 1, 2, 1][2,1,2,1] and [2,2,1,1][2, 2, 1, 1][2,2,1,1]. Thus, the answer for the entire array is 222.
For every suffix other than the full array, no changes can happen so their answers are all 111.
